# Skill: Social Media Marketing -- Research-Backed Strategy for Farm Brands

## Purpose
Reference library and operational framework for F.A.Y.E.'s social media marketing domain. These peer-reviewed sources, industry reports, and platform-specific guidelines inform how F.A.Y.E. advises on social media strategy, content creation, account management, influencer partnerships, AI-assisted content, and multi-platform operations for GreenReach farms and the broader network.

## Scope
This skill covers the research and practice of social media marketing as it applies to small-to-medium agricultural businesses:
- Platform selection and prioritisation based on usage and discovery data
- Content strategy grounded in quality, interaction, and analytics rather than posting volume
- Short-form video tactics and audiovisual characteristics that drive engagement
- Influencer and creator partnerships: credibility over follower count
- AI-assisted content creation: benefits, risks, and disclosure considerations
- Social media as a customer care channel, not just a broadcasting tool
- Account creation, security, and team management for each major platform
- Platform-specific roles: YouTube for search, Instagram and TikTok for discovery, Facebook for community, LinkedIn for B2B

## Recommended Reading Order
For building F.A.Y.E.'s social media intelligence:
1. Bartoloni et al. (2024) -- 20-year systematic review (foundational landscape)
2. Pushparaj et al. (2024) -- influencer marketing mechanisms
3. Meng et al. (2024) -- short-form video content characteristics
4. Zhang et al. (2025) -- audiovisual features and engagement
5. Bruns et al. (2024) -- AI-generated social content detection
6. Park et al. (2024) -- AI disclosure and engagement intention
7. Chen et al. (2025) -- automated content and credibility
8. Teepapal et al. (2025) -- AI-driven personalisation and engagement

For practical planning, keep these current industry documents as context:
9. Pew Research social media use fact sheets -- platform selection data
10. DataReportal 2025 -- platform use and brand research behaviour
11. Sprout Social 2025 Index -- customer care and content expectations
12. Deloitte Digital 2025 State of Social -- commerce and discovery trends

---

## Reference Library

### 1. Social Media Marketing Foundations

**Bartoloni, Nannelli, Marchini, Ferriani (2024)**
"Twenty years of social media marketing knowledge: a systematic review and research agenda."
Systematic review covering two decades of SMM research. The central finding is that results come from the combination of content quality, interaction, and analytics, not from posting volume alone. Stronger social media marketing capabilities are linked to higher brand equity and better firm performance, while engagement-rich activity can increase trust, loyalty, and value co-creation. For F.A.Y.E.: when advising farms on social media, prioritise content quality and audience interaction over posting frequency. A farm that posts three strong pieces per week with genuine engagement will outperform one that posts daily without interaction.

---

### 2. Short-Form Video Content

Short-form video is one of the strongest-backed content tactics in current social media research.

**Meng et al. (2024)**
Research on short-form video ad characteristics.
Found that trustworthiness, expertise, and attractiveness tend to improve outcomes, while authenticity matters in a more nuanced way rather than as a simple "more is always better" variable. For F.A.Y.E.: when advising on video content, prioritise demonstrating genuine expertise (growing knowledge, harvest technique, food safety) and trustworthiness (real farm footage, real results) over polished production value. Authenticity should feel natural, not performed.

**Zhang et al. (2025)**
Follow-up research on audiovisual features in short-form video.
Found that colloquial language, cadence, colourfulness, and visual prominence can affect engagement. For F.A.Y.E.: recommend that farm video content use natural spoken language (not scripted corporate tone), maintain visual variety (close-ups of plants, time-lapse growth, harvest action), and use colourful, well-lit footage. These specific audiovisual properties are measurably linked to engagement, not just aesthetic preferences.

---

### 3. Influencer and Creator Marketing

**Pushparaj et al. (2024)**
Systematic review of influencer marketing.
The literature points to credibility, relatability, and trust as more important than follower count by itself. Influencer marketing can improve purchase intention and follower growth, but it works best when the creator feels credible and relevant to the audience rather than merely famous. For F.A.Y.E.: when recommending influencer partnerships for farms, prioritise local food creators, chef accounts, community garden influencers, and food educators over generic lifestyle influencers with large followings. A local food blogger with 2,000 engaged followers in the farm's delivery area is more valuable than a national account with 200,000 followers and no local connection.

---

### 4. AI-Assisted Content Creation

The evidence supports AI as a useful tool that requires careful application.

**Bruns et al. (2024)**
Research on AI-generated social content and detection.
People struggle to distinguish AI-created accounts from human ones. For F.A.Y.E.: this cuts both ways. AI can help produce consistent, professional content, but if an audience discovers content was fully AI-generated without disclosure, trust damage can be significant. Always recommend human review and editing of AI-generated drafts.

**Park et al. (2024)**
Research on AI disclosure and engagement intention.
Disclosure of AI involvement can change engagement intentions. For F.A.Y.E.: when farms use AI tools for content, advise transparent but proportionate disclosure. For AI-assisted drafting, no disclosure is typically needed. For fully AI-generated content presented as human voice, disclosure is both ethically appropriate and practically safer for maintaining trust.

**Chen et al. (2025)**
Research on automated content and credibility.
When brands automate social content creation too heavily, follower reactions can weaken because credibility and authenticity drop. For F.A.Y.E.: recommend AI for idea generation, repurposing, headline testing, caption drafts, comment triage, and audience analysis, but keep a human in charge of final wording, claims, and tone. The research supports AI as an enhancement tool, not a substitute for brand judgment.

**Teepapal et al. (2025)**
Research on AI-driven personalisation and engagement.
AI-driven personalisation can improve engagement, but privacy and trust concerns matter. For F.A.Y.E.: recommend using AI personalisation for content targeting and audience segmentation, but advise farms to be transparent about data use and to avoid personalisation that feels intrusive or surveillance-like. A "What's growing this week near you" email is welcomed; "We noticed you haven't ordered since Tuesday" can feel invasive.

---

### 5. Social Media as Customer Care

Social media now functions as both marketing and customer care.

**Sprout Social 2025 Index (industry report)**
Consumers increasingly expect brands to respond on social within about a day. Users are less impressed by trend-chasing than by useful, original, responsive content. For F.A.Y.E.: advise farms to treat comments and direct messages as part of marketing, not an afterthought. Prompt replies, especially to questions, complaints, and buying signals, support trust and performance more than simply publishing more posts. A farm that responds to every comment within 4 hours but posts twice a week will build stronger customer relationships than one that posts daily but ignores comments.

---

### 6. Platform Usage and Discovery Patterns

**Pew Research social media use fact sheets**
YouTube and Facebook remain the most widely used platforms in the U.S. For F.A.Y.E.: these are the two platforms with the broadest reach for any farm audience, regardless of demographic.

**DataReportal 2025**
Facebook remains the most widely self-reported social platform globally. Instagram, Facebook, and TikTok are leading channels people use to research brands and products. For F.A.Y.E.: when a consumer is deciding whether to buy from a local farm, they are most likely to check Instagram, Facebook, or TikTok. These platforms need to present clear, current, trust-building content.

**Deloitte Digital 2025 State of Social**
Commerce and discovery are increasingly social-native. For F.A.Y.E.: social media is not just awareness -- it is a direct pathway to purchase decisions. Content should make it easy for a viewer to understand what the farm sells, how to order, and where to find the product.

---

## Platform Strategy Framework

### Core Stack (Open First)
The best core stack for most businesses matches current usage and discovery patterns:

| Platform | Primary Role | Content Focus |
|---|---|---|
| YouTube | Evergreen, searchable content | Farm tours, growing guides, harvest walkthroughs, equipment reviews, seasonal planning |
| Facebook | Community, local promotion, customer touchpoints | Events, market schedules, weekly availability, customer interaction, local partnerships |
| Instagram | Discovery and short-form visual proof | Behind-the-scenes, harvest photos, Reels (short-form video), Stories for daily updates |
| TikTok | Discovery and short-form proof | Quick harvests, growing tips, farm day-in-the-life, trending audio + farm content |
| LinkedIn | B2B partnerships, hiring, thought leadership | Restaurant partnerships, wholesale programme announcements, CEA industry posts, hiring |

### Secondary Platform
| Platform | Role | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| X | Real-time news, commentary, press visibility | Announcements, live market commentary, founder voice, industry events |

### Content Mix
The strongest content mix across all platforms:

**Educational proof** (40-50% of content):
- Growing demos, harvest technique
- Before/after crop progression
- FAQs about indoor farming, food safety, freshness
- Behind-the-scenes of daily farm operations
- Seasonal growing guides and crop spotlights

**Social proof** (25-35% of content):
- Customer stories and testimonials
- Restaurant and chef partner mentions
- User-generated content from customers
- Creator collaborations with local food influencers
- Community event participation

**Commercial proof** (15-25% of content):
- New crop launches and seasonal availability
- Special offers and subscription promotions
- Market and delivery schedule updates
- Product explanations (why microgreens, why local, why indoor-grown)
- Event announcements (farm tours, tastings, market days)

This structure matches the research on trustworthiness, expertise, authenticity, and creator credibility.

### AI Usage Guidelines
Use AI for:
- Idea generation and content calendar planning
- Repurposing long-form content into platform-specific formats
- Headline and caption draft writing
- Comment triage and response prioritisation
- Audience analysis and engagement pattern detection

Keep human control over:
- Final wording on all published content
- Health, safety, and nutrition claims
- Brand voice and tone decisions
- Creator partnership selection
- Customer complaint responses
- Financial or pricing disclosures

---

## Account Creation and Security

### Facebook
- Create a business presence as a **Page**, not a personal profile
- Manage through **Meta Business Suite** (free tool for Facebook, Instagram, and Messenger)
- Use access controls and role-based permissions rather than sharing a single password
- Enable **two-factor authentication** in Accounts Center

### Instagram
- Create a new account or convert an existing one into a **professional account** (business or creator)
- Professional accounts unlock business tools, insights, monetisation options, and ad features
- Secure with **two-factor authentication** through Accounts Center, ideally with an authentication app and backup codes
- For team use, rely on shared/business access tools rather than sharing a password

### LinkedIn
- Create a **Company Page** from the "For Business" menu (requires a LinkedIn account)
- Creator becomes super admin and can assign others as admins
- LinkedIn provides **tiered admin roles** for different access levels
- LinkedIn explicitly recommends **two-factor authentication** for Pages; Business Manager requires 2FA

### YouTube
- Create a channel with a **business or other name** (Brand Account) so multiple people can manage it
- Channel permissions are safer than sharing a password and allow different access levels
- Turn on **Google 2-Step Verification** for the owner account

### TikTok
- Create a **Business Account** (designed for businesses wanting marketing tools and audience growth features)
- **TikTok Studio** includes analytics, viewer/follower insights, engagement insights, and security settings
- Enable **2-step verification** and trusted devices

### X
- Create an account on X.com or in the app
- Enable **two-factor authentication** for account security
- Treat X as an add-on for announcements, live commentary, press visibility, and founder voice rather than a primary channel

### Best Sign-In and Management Setup
The safest operating model for any platform:
- One owner account with 2FA enabled
- Team members added through roles and permissions
- No shared passwords
- One central place to manage publishing and inboxes where possible (Meta Business Suite, LinkedIn admin view, YouTube Studio/channel permissions, TikTok Studio)

---

## Applicability to F.A.Y.E.

| F.A.Y.E. Domain | Relevant Research |
|---|---|
| Social Media Strategy Planning | Bartoloni 2024 (quality + interaction + analytics > volume) |
| Content Calendar and Mix | Platform Strategy Framework (educational / social / commercial proof ratios) |
| Short-Form Video Guidance | Meng 2024 (trustworthiness, expertise), Zhang 2025 (audiovisual features) |
| Creator and Influencer Partnerships | Pushparaj 2024 (credibility and relevance > follower count) |
| AI Content Assistance | Bruns 2024, Park 2024, Chen 2025, Teepapal 2025 (AI = enhancement, not substitute) |
| Customer Care via Social | Sprout Social 2025 (response time, useful content > trend-chasing) |
| Platform Selection | Pew Research, DataReportal 2025 (YouTube + Facebook broadest; Instagram + TikTok for brand research) |
| Account Security and Team Management | Platform-specific sections (2FA, role-based access, no shared passwords) |
| Farm Network Marketing | Per-farm social presence guidance, network-level brand consistency |
| GreenReach-Specific Planning | Framework for account priorities, bio text, posting pillars, weekly workflow |

## Rules
- Currency is always CAD.
- No emojis in any output.
- Do not fabricate engagement metrics, follower counts, or platform statistics.
- Recommend AI as an enhancement tool, not a substitute for human brand judgment.
- Always advise two-factor authentication and role-based access for all platform accounts.
- Prioritise content quality and audience interaction over posting volume.
- Prioritise local, credible creator partnerships over high-follower-count influencers.
- Treat comments and direct messages as marketing activity, not an afterthought.
- When advising on video content, reference specific research-backed characteristics (trustworthiness, expertise, colloquial language, visual prominence).
- Use Canadian English (colour, favourite, centre).
